coredata.dc:title: Bimetallic AuCu nanoclusters-based florescent chemosensor for sensitive detection of Fe3+ in environmental and biological systems
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Shojaeifard, Zahra
Heidari, Nahid
Hemmateenejad, Bahram
coredata.dc:description: Assays of ferric ion (Fe3+) with high sensitivity and selectivity have been required to evaluate its amount in environmental and biological systems. Herein, a novel fluorometric penicillamine-capped bimetallic gold-copper nanoclusters (PA-AuCu bi-MNCs) sensor was constructed for facile, environmentally friendly and quantitative detection of Fe3+ through inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism. One-step green synthetic approach was applied for the synthesis of AuCu bi-MNCs by using d-penicillamine (D-PA) as template and stabilizer. In the presence of Fe3+, the emission of the PA-AuCu bi-MNCs was hindered that caused selective quenching of the fluorescence intensity. The response to Fe3+ allows for two linear dynamic ranges of 5.0 × 10−7 M–7.0 × 10−6 M and 7.0 × 10−6 M–1.0 × 10−4 M with a detection limit of 0.1 μM, which is approximately 53 times lower than the maximum level (5.37 μM) of Fe3+ in drinking water that had been reported by the World Health Organization. The independency of the system from most of the interferences is the important feature of this work. Beside the appropriate selectivity of the proposed method, it shows a considerable operation in various environmental samples including rain water, three types of river water and also in human blood serum as a biological matrix.
coredata.dcterms:subject: AuCu nanoclusters
Bimetallic nanoclusters
Fluorescence
Ferric ions
Sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure for Fluorescent Detection of Fe3+
Characterization of the Synthesized PA-AuCu Bi-MNCs
Effect of Volume of PA-AuCu Bi-MNCs Solution
Sensitivity of the PA-AuCu Bi-MNCs Toward Fe3+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of PA-AuCu bi-MNCs (B) FT-IR spectra of D-PA and PA-AuCu bi-MNCs (C) UV–Vis absorption of PA-AuCu bi-MNCs (D) FL emission spectra of PA-AuCu bi-MNCs when excited at 275 nm. (Insets (C) and (D) are digital photos of solutions of the PA-AuCu bi-MNCs under visible and UV irradiation at <365 nm.)

Changes in FL emission spectra of (a) 70 μL PA-AuCu bi-MNCs in the presence of (b) 10.0 μM and (c) 100.0 μM of Fe3+ solution. The FL spectra were recorded upon excitation at 275 nm in UBS with pH 3.0. (Inset is digital photos of (a) PA-AuCu bi-MNCs, (b) PA-AuCu bi-MNCs + 10.0 μM Fe3+ and (c) PA-AuCu bi-MNCs + 100.0 μM Fe3+ under UV light with excitation wavelength at 360 nm).

UV–Vis absorbance spectra of (A) PA-AuCu bi-MNCs in the presence of varying concentrations of Fe3+ (1–4: 0, 2, 8, 10 μM.) (B) (a) PA-AuCu bi-MNCs, (b) PA-AuCu bi-MNCs and Phen, (c) PA-AuCu bi-MNCs, Phen and Fe3+ and (d) PA-AuCu bi-MNCs, Phen and Fe2+. The volume and concentrations of PA-AuCu bi-MNCs, Phen and Fe3+ were 100 μL, 10 μM and 500 μM respectively (C) (a) PA-AuCu MNCs, (b) Fe3+ and (c) Fe2+. The volume and concentration of PA-AuCu bi-MNCs, Fe3+ and Fe2+ were 100 μL, 50 μM and 50 μM, respectively. (UBS with pH 3.0).

(A) FL emission spectra of PA-AuCu bi-MNCs upon addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ (1–17: 0, 0.5, 0.7, 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120 and 130 μM). (B) The linear calibration plot between ΔF/F0 ((F0 − F) / F0) and the concentration of Fe3+ (0.5–100.0 μM). All data were collected from three measurements, and the error bars indicated the standard deviation (n = 3). The FL spectra were recorded upon a 275 nm excitation in UBS pH 3.0.
The ΔF/F0 of PA-AuCu bi-MNCs upon the addition of 200.0 μM of various anions and cations to the solution containing 10.0 μM of Fe3 +.
Comparison with other reported fluorimetric based methods for the detection of Fe3+.

row.entry: Polyethyleneimine-templated ascorbic acid capped Cu NCs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorometric detection of Fe3+ in Fe3+-spiked real samples by the PA-AuCu bi-MNCs probe.

head.ce:title: Bimetallic AuCu nanoclusters-based florescent chemosensor for sensitive detection of Fe3+ in environmental and biological systems

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Shiraz University, Shiraz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Medicinal and Natural Products Chemistry Research Center, Shiraz University of Medical Sciences, Shiraz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Medicinal and Natural Products Chemistry Research Center

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Chemistry Department, Shiraz University, Shiraz, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Assays of ferric ion (Fe3+) with high sensitivity and selectivity have been required to evaluate its amount in environmental and biological systems. Herein, a novel fluorometric penicillamine-capped bimetallic gold-copper nanoclusters (PA-AuCu bi-MNCs) sensor was constructed for facile, environmentally friendly and quantitative detection of Fe3+ through inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism. One-step green synthetic approach was applied for the synthesis of AuCu bi-MNCs by using d-penicillamine (D-PA) as template and stabilizer. In the presence of Fe3+, the emission of the PA-AuCu bi-MNCs was hindered that caused selective quenching of the fluorescence intensity. The response to Fe3+ allows for two linear dynamic ranges of 5.0 × 10−7 M–7.0 × 10−6 M and 7.0 × 10−6 M–1.0 × 10−4 M with a detection limit of 0.1 μM, which is approximately 53 times lower than the maximum level (5.37 μM) of Fe3+ in drinking water that had been reported by the World Health Organization. The independency of the system from most of the interferences is the important feature of this work. Beside the appropriate selectivity of the proposed method, it shows a considerable operation in various environmental samples including rain water, three types of river water and also in human blood serum as a biological matrix.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal ions are involved in many significant biological, environmental, nanotechnology and catalysis processes [1]. Among them, ferric ion (Fe3+) is known as the most essential transition metal ion in life system. From biological point, it is one of the important cofactors in various cellular metabolism reactions. Many physiological processes such as oxygen metabolism, oxygen transportation, and transcriptional regulation required the presence of Fe3+ for their proficiency [2]. In particular, it is present in hem proteins promote the formation of red blood cells. So the lack of Fe3+ can lead to anemia [3]. However, excess amount of Fe3+ can lead to iron poisoning by catalyzing the generation of reactive oxygen species and causes serious disease (cancer, Parkinson and Alzheimer) [4].

Fe3+ play crucial role in natural water systems. It is an important element in the plant metabolism, photosynthesis, and nitrate reduction [5]. Moreover, Fe3+ is an important parameter in oceanography studies. Indirectly, low concentrations of iron effects on the regulation of the global climate via the reduction of the oceanic phytoplankton ability to descend atmospheric CO2 [6]. Industrial processes have been known as another major sources of pollutants which constantly released iron ions into the nature environment that have been caused serious environmental and health problems [7]. However, the lack and overload of Fe3+ causes the biological and environmental problems. According to the importance of the Fe3+, it is greatly demanded to develop a selective and sensitive method for the determination of Fe3+ in various matrices.

Until now, various analytical techniques have been proposed for the detection and determination of Fe3+ including inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [8], voltammetry [9], atomic absorption spectroscopy [10,11], colorimetric [12] and fluorescent method [13]. Among the proposed methods, fluorometric assay attracted many attentions due to its ease of operation, high sensitivity, efficiency, and affordable [14,15]. The most of current successful fluorescent probes for Fe3+ detection are limited to organic dyes [16], quantum dots [17], and Cdots [18]. However, most of the probes based on organic molecules have complicated synthesis, poor photostability and low solubility in aqueous solution [19,20]. Also, the hard synthesis procedure and toxicity effect of quantum dots made it as an inappropriate candidate for fluorescence (FL) determination of Fe3+ [21,22]. Therefore, it is still a challenge to design a facile synthesized, photostable, selective, and environmental friendly nanosensors for detection of Fe3+.

In recent years, metal nanoclusters (MNCs) have been introduced as a promising class of fluorophores that its similar size-dependent bandgap and quantum confinement effects made it as a desirable substitute for toxic quantum dots [23]. Noble MNCs (Ag, Au and Cu) with molecule-like properties possess quantum size effect, catalytic activity, high photoluminescence and photostability. Each NC with diverse capping agents solely showed remarkable behavior in the detection of various biomolecules [24–26] and ions [27–29]. Besides, bimetallic NCs have been applied as another type of nanoprobes. They attracted attention because of synergistic effect from two kinds of metal atoms that caused better catalytic activity, strong luminescence and higher sensitivity [30,31]. Also, metal-metal interactions (metallophilicity) which are generally observed in metals that contain closed-shell d10 ions/atoms, often lead to change luminescence properties [32,33]. This strategy has been successfully used to synthesize luminescent bimetallic NCs.

Here, we tried to apply the notable metallophilicity and so remarkable photoluminescence that is between Au-Cu clusters to design a FL sensor. In this regard, penicillamine-capped AuCu bimetallic NCs (PA-AuCu bi-MNCs) was applied for the detection of Fe3+ based on the FL quenching effect of Fe3+. After investigating all possible quenching mechanisms, it has been proved that inner filter effect (IFE) is the main reason through shielding effect of Fe3+. Accordingly, the PA-AuCu bi-MNCs can be applied for designing a sensitive and cost-effective probe for the monitoring of Fe3+ in environmental and biological real samples.

All FL spectra were recorded on a Perkin-Elmer LS-50 Spectrofluorometer equipped with a water bath. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were made on a TEM (Zeiss, EM10C) operated at 80 kV. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a FTIR-8300 Shimadzo spectrophotometer. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy analysis was taken using a TESCAN-Vega 3 instrument.

Iron(III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), citric acid ((HOOCCH2)2C(OH)COOH), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), phosphoric acid (H3PO4) and acetic acid (CH3COOH) were obtained from Merck Chemicals company. Boric acid (H3BO3) was purchased from BDH. d-Penicillamine (C5H11NO2S) and 1,10 phenanthroline (C12H8N2), were purchased from Fluka. Throughout all parts of our experiments, double-deionized water was used. All chemicals and reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

The universal buffer solutions (UBS) over the pH range of 2.0–7.0 were prepared by successive addition of different amounts of 0.2 M NaOH to 1:1:1 aqueous mixture of boric acid (H3BO3), phosphoric acid (H3PO4) and acetic acid (CH3COOH) (all 0.05 M).

Stock solutions of Fe3+ (2.0 × 10−2 M) were prepared by dissolving an appropriate amount of FeCl3.6H2O in water. Lower concentrations of Fe3+ were prepared by dilution of the stock standard solution with water. All the solutions were prepared and used freshly. It should be mentioned that in the prepared stock solutions of Fe3+ any precipitates of Fe(OH)3 was not observed.

The AuCu bi-MNCs were synthesized by chemical reduction of HAuCl4 and Cu(NO3)2 with d-penicillamine (PA) according to the procedure described previously by Chen et al. [34]. In brief, aqueous solution of PA (30.0 mM, 900.0 μL) was added into the HAuCl4 (10.0 mM, 50.0 μL) under vigorous stirring. As the next step, 50.0 μL aqueous solution of 0.1 M nitric acid containing 5.0 mM Cu(NO3)2 was added. The reaction mixture was stirred for 2 h at ambient temperature in dark to obtain a white milky solution. To purify the synthesized NCs, the product was collected with centrifugation at 2900 rpm. Finally, the collected sediment was re-dispersed in water (1.0 mL).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for Fluorescent Detection of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: For investigating the quenching effect of Fe3+, a 70.0 μL aliquot of purified PA-AuCu bi-MNCs was mixed with various concentrations of Fe3+ (5.0 × 10−7–1.0 × 10−4 M). The mixture was diluted to 2.0 mL with 0.05 M buffer solution of pH = 3. After incubating for 5 min, the FL spectra were recorded in the range of 570–700 nm (with excitation at 275 nm). A scan speed of 500 nm min−1 and slit width of 10 nm were chosen for all FL measurements. At the end, the FL intensity was quantified to construct the calibration curve of Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the Synthesized PA-AuCu Bi-MNCs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of PA-AuCu bi-MNCs was accomplished as reported earlier [34]. Among various choices for selection of a desire NCs with appropriate capping agent, AuCu bi-MNCs stabilized with PA were picked as the qualified FL probe. Here, Cu is getting along with Au that produced NCs with long lifetime and facile synthetic routes without needing any toxic chemical reducing agent (hydrazine hydrate and sodium borohydride) and any laborious purification procedures [35]. Also, PA as a therapeutic substance and biocompatible chelating agent possess carboxyl, amino and mercapto groups that help the strong interaction of heavy metal ions by coordination with its acidic and basic functional groups. Moreover, PA is known as a selective chelator for Cu ions [36]. This feature is effectively applied in the clinical management to eliminate excess amount of Cu in tissue and also in synthesis procedures for modification of nanostructures especially Cu nanostructures [37–39]. As Chen and his co-workers mentioned for the proposed PA-AuCu bi-MNCs synthesis rout [34], PA have three major roles in the reactions; forming PA-Au(I)Cu(I) complexes through a strong d10 metallophilic interaction between the two metal ions, reducing the PA-Au(I)Cu(I) complexes and finally stabilizing the AuCu NCs [34].

According to the TEM image shown in Fig. 1A, highly dispersed synthesized NCs with distinct boundaries implied the formation of alloy metal NCs. The average size of the PA-AuCu bi-MNCs is about 2.5 nm (±0.1), obtained by determining the size of 52 particles that is similar to the previous report [34]. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) analysis supports the presence of both Au and Cu in the bi-MNCs (as shown in Supporting Information Fig. S1). Moreover, FTIR spectroscopy was applied for studying the surface structure of bi-MNCs. Fig. 1B compares the FTIR of free D-PA and bi-MNCs. As it is obvious, the band at 2527 cm−1 related to S-H stretching band of free DPA almost disappeared after conjugation on the surface of bi-MNCs. It revealed that the SH moiety have an important role in the reduction of the Cu2+ and Au3+ species. Also, the presence of carboxylate (COO−) and primary amino (NH2) groups of D-PA on the surface of bi-MNCs are confirmed by observing the characteristic peaks of stretch modes of COO– (1388 and 1494 cm−1), NH stretch (3410 cm−1) and NH bending (1618 cm−1) of NH2.

The optical properties of PA-AuCu bi-MNCs were investigated using UV–Vis absorption and FL spectroscopies. As shown in the Fig. 1C, bi-MNCs represent a broad absorption band maximum at around 275 nm [34]. The absence of any absorbance spectra at around 560–600 nm confirms the lack of Cu nanoparticles [40]. The FL measurements showed that PA-AuCu bi-MNCs had an emission peak at 605 nm when excited at the 275 nm (Fig. 1D). The aqueous solution of PA-AuCu bi-MNCs was milky white color under visible light and exhibited an orange emission FL under the UV lamp at 365 nm (inset of Fig. 1C and D, respectively).

In addition, the quantum yields (QY) of the luminescent PA-AuCu bi-MNCs in the absence and presence of the Fe3+ (40 μM) were determined by comparison with the known QY of tyrosine at λex = 275 nm. The QYs were determined to be 3.5% and 2.6% for PA-AuCu bi-MNCs and PA-AuCu bi-MNCs in the presence of Fe3+ (60 μM), respectively.

In the presence of Fe3+, the FL emission of PA-AuCu bi-MNCs can be efficiently quenched. As it is shown in Fig. 2 , addition of the Fe3+ (10.0 and 100.0 μM) to the PA-AuCu bi-MNCs solution causes a decreasing in the FL intensity of the NCs around 605 nm with a slight shift of the maximum emission wavelength to longer wavelengths. FL quenching can be referred to various mechanisms, including dynamic quenching (electron transfer processes), static quenching (formation of a ground-state non-fluorescent complex), energy transfer processes, molecular rearrangements, and also IFE or non-radiative recombination pathway [41]. In order to get insight of the quenching mechanism of PA-AuCu bi-MNCs by Fe3+, we analyze all the possible quenching.

The dynamic quenching is due to the diffusion of quencher to the fluorophore in the excited state while static quenching comes from a non-fluorescent complex formation between quencher and fluorophore [42]. A common method for distinguishing between these mechanisms is monitoring changes in the absorbance spectra of the fluorescent species in the presence of quencher. In the dynamic quenching, quencher affects the excited state of the fluorophore and hence no variation in the absorbance spectra is expected. However, for static quenching, ground-state complex formation considerably influences on the absorption spectra of the FL substance. In order to investigate the quenching mechanism, the UV–Vis spectra of PA-AuCu MNCs in the absence and presence of Fe3+ were studied. As shown in Fig. 3A, no shift in the maxima absorbance of PA-AuCu bi-MNCs was observed after addition of Fe3+ ion. Therefore, it can be concluded that the ground state complex between Fe3+ ions and PA-AuCu bi-MNCs is not formed.

Another possible quenching mechanism is electron transfer processes. The electron transfer mechanism was further supported by the following experiments. Generally, 1,10-phenanthroline (Phen) is used to investigate the existence of Fe2+ ions in the system in terms of the appearance of a characteristic absorbance band at 508 nm attributed to the complex [Fe(Phen)3]2+ [43]. Thus, Phen was added to the mixture of the PA-AuCu MNCs and Fe3+ to testify the occurrence of Fe2+ in the process of FL quenching. As shown in Fig. 3B(c), any absorbance band at 508 nm was not observed which showed the absence of any Fe2+. So the probability of an electron transfer mechanism is unlikely. Nevertheless, if Phen was added to the mixture of the PA-AuCu MNCs and Fe2+, the absorbance band at 508 nm was observed (Fig. 3B(d)).

Another noticeable mechanism of many fluorometric procedures is the absorption of exciting and/or emitted radiation by quenchers. This phenomenon that called the inner filter effect (IFE) occurs when the absorption spectrum of quencher has a significant spectral overlap with emission and/or excitation spectra of fluorophore [44]. As shown in Fig. 3C, the absorbance spectrum of PA-AuCu bi-MNCs has a band at 275 nm. In addition, Fe3+ exhibits absorbance around 275 nm that showed quite overlapping with the excitation band of PA-AuCu bi-MNCs. Consequently, Fe3+ can shield the excitation light of PA-AuCu bi-MNCs. Therefore, the absorbance enhancement of Fe3+ caused to the FL quenching of PA-AuCu bi-MNCs. Based on these results, it can be concluded that the probable reason for FL quenching of PA-AuCu bi-MNCs in the presence of Fe3+ is IFE. Since the absorbance of Fe2+ at 275 nm is too weak, Fe2+ has no interference on Fe3+ determination (Fig. 3C). In addition, it should be note that Fe3+ and Fe2+ get from salts of FeCl3 and FeCl2, respectively, and Cl− anions have no effect on absorbance of FeCl3 solution.

As it is shown in Fig. 2, by addition of the Fe3+ to the PA-AuCu bi-MNCs solution, a decrease in FL emission of PA-AuCu bi-MNCs with a slight red shift of the maximum emission wavelength was observed. This reduction in FL intensity was used as a base for designing an appropriate analytical probe for the determination of Fe3+. The analytical signal was defined as relative FL different (ΔF/F0). ΔF/F0 was calculated as (F0 − F) / F0 where F0 and F are the FL emission intensity of PA-AuCu bi-MNCs before and after addition of Fe3+ at 605 nm (excitation wavelength is 275 nm), respectively. In order to gain higher sensitivity, the reaction conditions including amounts of the PA-AuCu bi-MNCs, pH, type of buffer solutions, buffer concentration and reaction time were optimized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Volume of PA-AuCu Bi-MNCs Solution
ce:section.ce:para: During experiments, it was found that the FL intensity of PA-AuCu MNCs is decreased spontaneously when the NC volumes is higher than 100.0 μL. In fact, this phenomenon can be related to the self-absorption of PA-AuCu MNCs at high concentrations. Also, at the low volumes (<50.0 μL), the FL intensity of PA-AuCu MNCs was not enough to be used in measurements. With these explanations, three different amounts of MNCs including 50.0, 70.0 and 100.0 μM were chosen. According to Fig. S2, at a volume of 70 and 100.0 μL, the PA-AuCu NCs represent appropriate FL quenching in the presence of 1.0 × 10−5 M Fe3+. To make sure that self-quenching has not been occurred, 70.0 μL of MNCs was chosen as optimum volume.

Generally, the pH values of investigated solutions have great effect on the detection of targets. Fe3+ has a cationic form in acidic solutions, while in basic solutions, the formation of Fe (OH)3 hinder the ability of desired ion for further interactions. On the other hand, our selected probe, PA-AuCu bi-MNCs, is stable at lower pH values due to the occurrence of aggregate induced enhancement mechanism at these pH values [34]. As shown in Fig. S3, pH 3 has the best intensity and stability due to the formation of hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions among PA-Au(I)Cu(I) complexes in the acidic solutions. In addition, the electrostatic repulsion increased by increasing pH values. In this regard, pH 3.0 was chosen for all experiments.

To select the best kind of buffer solution in pH 3, the proficiency of the PA-AuCu bi-MNCs was studied in different types of buffer solutions including acetate-phosphate buffer, citrate buffer and UBS media in the presence of 1.0 × 10−6 M of Fe3+. It can be concluded from Fig. S4 that the most acceptable ΔF/F0 was achieved in the UBS at the applied experimental conditions.

The buffer concentration was also an effective parameter for detection of target. For this goal, the quenching of FL in the presence of 1.0 × 10−6 M Fe3+ was studied for four different concentrations of UBS (0.01 M, 0.05 M, 0.1 M and 0.2 M). As can be seen from Fig. S5, the ΔF/F0 is in its maximum state in 0.05 M UBS.

Fig. S6 presents the response time of PA-AuCu bi-MNCs toward Fe3+. The results for 1.0 μM and 10.0 μM solutions of Fe3+ showed that the ΔF/F0 had being stable and achieved a platform after 5 min. Accordingly, incubation time for sensing of Fe3+ was taken as 5 min after addition of sample solutions to the reagent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of the PA-AuCu Bi-MNCs Toward Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Quantitative detection of Fe3+ was carried out in UBS (0.05 M, pH 3). As shown in Fig. 4A, FL intensity of PA-AuCu bi-MNCs were decreased gradually by increasing concentration of Fe3+. Besides, there was linear relations between the ΔF/F0 at 605 nm and the concentration of Fe3+in two linear dynamic ranges from 5.0 × 10−7 M to 7.0 × 10−6 M and 7.0 × 10−6 M to 1.0 × 10−4 M (Fig. 4B).

By using the proposed fluorometric sensor, the experimental detection limit of the method was found to be 1.0 × 10−7 M which is ~53 times lower than the permitted level of Fe3+ in drinking water (5.37 μM) that had been reported by the World Health Organization (WHO). Table 1 shows a comparison between the analytical characteristics of previously reported sensors and the proposed probe for the determination of Fe3+. Comparison confirmed that PA-AuCu bi-MNCs probe could realize the wide linear range of Fe3+ with good sensitivity.

To evaluate the specificity of the PA-AuCu bi-MNCs probe for the detection and determination of Fe3+, the anti-interferential capability of designed probe was studied in the presence of other competitive ions. In this regard, 22 ions including different anions and cations, such as K+, Na+, Se4+, Al3+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Li+, S2−, Br−, F−, and Cl−, were tested in the presence of 1.0 × 10−5 M Fe3+ when their concentrations were 20 times of the Fe3+ amount (Fig. 5 ). Beside considerable specificity of bi-MNCs sensing system toward different ions, its excellent selectivity of the proposed sensor against Fe2+ as a common Fe3+ interference is remarkable that can be related to mechanism of quenching i.e. the inner filter effect of Fe3+.

To determine the practicality of the designed probe, the PA-AuCu bi-MNCs sensor was used to detect Fe3+ in two different matrices. As the first real matrix, four types of water (i.e., rain water and diverse river water) were selected. Rainwater samples were collected manually on a time in pre-cleaned glass bottles for different rainfall hours. Samples of river water were collected from Zighan, Katdiouyeh and Dehrom River in Galedar, Darab and Firuzabad (Iran). The pH of water samples was adjusted to pH 3.0 by the addition of UBS components and NaOH, then the resultant solution was filtered by Whatman filter paper (40 m). Human blood serum, as the second real matrix was used after deproteination by 20% trichloroacetic acid according to previously reported method [13]. To lower down the matrix effect, the serum samples were diluted seventy times with BRB (pH 3). Standard addition method was applied for Fe3+ determination in spiked water samples and human blood serum. As shown in Tables 2 , the results demonstrate satisfactory recoveries for determination of Fe3+ in the five real samples. These results confirmed that the proposed sensing system has great potential for quantitative analysis of Fe3+ levels in environmental and biological samples.

In summary, by applying PA-AuCu bi-MNCs as a group of chemical compound that show strong luminescence properties, we have developed a fluorescent sensor for the sensitive detection of Fe3+. The FL emission of PA-AuCu bi-MNCs as our designed probe was significantly quenched upon the addition of Fe3+ due to the IFE. The proposed bi-MNCs can be served as a highly sensitive probe for the determination of Fe3+ with a detection limit as low as 0.1 μM that is much lower than the permitted value by WHO. The present IFE-based strategy allows designing a simple, rapid and selective fluorescent assays without the interference effect of Fe2+. Moreover, the bi-MNCs probe showed considerable precision and accuracy when applied to detect Fe3+ in different environmental and biological samples. Its reliable operation in the river waters, rainwater and serum blood confirmed the efficiency of the proposed probe for the determination of Fe3+ in complex systems.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support of this work by Shiraz University Research Council is highly appreciated.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2018.10.042.
